channel.title: Posts on Nordic Numbers
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Nordic Numbers

item.title: Lillehammer 2025 Recap
item.description:
Introduction

Week two is done.

Race 1 (Distance Freestyle)

item.title: Cross-Country Skiing Season Simulation Results
item.description:
Men’s Results

Top 10 Current Standings (Men)

  1. Johannes Høsflot Klæbo (397 points)
  2. Harald Østberg Amundsen (528 points)
  3. Martin Løwstrøm Nyenget (401 points)
  4. Erik Valnes (256 points)
  5. Simen Hegstad Krüger (351 points)
  6. Pål Golberg (244 points)
  7. Iivo Niskanen (286 points)
  8. Federico Pellegrino (252 points)
  9. Jan Thomas Jenssen (256 points)
  10. Andreas Fjorden Ree (311 points)

Projected Final Top 10 (Men)

  1. Johannes Høsflot Klæbo (2622 points, 99.0% win probability)
  2. Harald Østberg Amundsen (2384 points, 1.0% win probability)
  3. Martin Løwstrøm Nyenget (2039 points, 0.0% win probability)
  4. Erik Valnes (1934 points, 0.0% win probability)
  5. Simen Hegstad Krüger (1831 points, 0.0% win probability)
  6. Pål Golberg (1822 points, 0.0% win probability)
  7. Iivo Niskanen (1576 points, 0.0% win probability)
  8. Federico Pellegrino (1561 points, 0.0% win probability)
  9. Jan Thomas Jenssen (1518 points, 0.0% win probability)
  10. Andreas Fjorden Ree (1386 points, 0.0% win probability)

Women’s Results

Top 10 Current Standings (Women)

  1. Jonna Sundling (438 points)
  2. Jessie Diggins (493 points)
  3. Therese Johaug (432 points)
  4. Frida Karlsson (231 points)
  5. Victoria Carl (432 points)
  6. Heidi Weng (394 points)
  7. Linn Svahn (151 points)
  8. Ebba Andersson (338 points)
  9. Kerttu Niskanen (228 points)
  10. Astrid Øyre Slind (318 points)

Projected Final Top 10 (Women)

  1. Jonna Sundling (2349 points, 65.4% win probability)
  2. Jessie Diggins (2298 points, 34.6% win probability)
  3. Therese Johaug (2088 points, 0.0% win probability)
  4. Frida Karlsson (2072 points, 0.0% win probability)
  5. Victoria Carl (1999 points, 0.0% win probability)
  6. Heidi Weng (1990 points, 0.0% win probability)
  7. Linn Svahn (1874 points, 0.0% win probability)
  8. Ebba Andersson (1799 points, 0.0% win probability)
  9. Kerttu Niskanen (1745 points, 0.0% win probability)
  10. Astrid Øyre Slind (1675 points, 0.0% win probability)

item.title: Lillehammer 2025 Predictions
item.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the second race of the season. For this weekend, watchout for Andreas Ree of Norway to continue to outpace predicitions.

Sources

Overall:

  • https://xcancel.com/FantasyXC

Canada

  • https://proxcskiing.com/traditional-skiing/

Czechia :

  • https://www.czech-ski.com/beh-na-lyzich/aktuality

  • https://www.facebook.com/@czeskisnowboard/

Finland:

  • svenska.yle.fi/a/7-10067960
  • https://yle.fi/a/74-
  • https://www.facebook.com/xcteamfinland

France

  • https://ffs.fr/actualites/

Germany

  • DSV Nordic Facebook Page (https://www.facebook.com/DSVNordisch)

item.title: Ruka 2025 Recap
item.description:
Introduction

Week one is done! Check out who did well and who was a turkey.

Race 1 (Distance Classic)

Skier Nation Before Elo After Elo Elo Difference Predicted Points Points Points Difference

item.title: Ruka 2025 Predictions
item.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the first race of the season. In this post and more to follow, I will present the likely starting list for the weekend as well as race by race predictions for odds as well as expected points

Sources

Overall:

  • https://xcancel.com/FantasyXC

Canada

  • https://proxcskiing.com/traditional-skiing/

Czechia :

  • https://www.czech-ski.com/beh-na-lyzich/aktuality

  • https://www.facebook.com/@czeskisnowboard/

Finland:

  • svenska.yle.fi/a/7-10067960
  • https://yle.fi/a/74-
  • https://www.facebook.com/xcteamfinland

France

  • https://ffs.fr/actualites/

Germany

  • DSV Nordic Facebook Page (https://www.facebook.com/DSVNordisch)

item.title: Scraping Ski Data for Statistical Analysis
item.description:
Goal: Getting the Data Needed to Perform Statistical Analysis

The first step to perform any meaningful statistical analysis is to get the data needed. Because I haven’t recorded ski results since the beginning of time, I need to use an external source. The seemingly obvious way to do this is to use the FIS website and search for World Cup races. While they undoubtedly have the highest volume of data and are the official arbiter of the results, their records management has something to be desired. Because our analysis will want to make sure that each individual skier is unique, names cannot be the ultimate form of identification since multiple people can have the same name. Instead, we need a unique ID for each skier. While FIS does have a FIS code, I discovered examples of people (especially women who got married) who have a FIS code for one name/spelling, and another FIS code for another name/spelling. On top of this, biathlon data is not stored on FIS, which would mean I would need a script to scrape from an IBU site.
